35: The destruction of the Assyrian army — wayhî ballaylāh hahûʾ wayyēṣēʾ malʾaḵ YHWH wayyaḵ bᵉmaḥănēh ʾaššûr ḥāmiššāh ûšᵉmōnîm ʾelep, ‘that night the angel of YHWH went out and struck down 185,000 in the camp of the Assyrians’ — is corroborated in a striking way by Herodotus (2.141): describing the same campaign, Herodotus reports that field mice invaded the Assyrian camp at night, eating the quivers, bowstrings, and shield-handles, rendering the army unable to fight. The Egyptian priestly tradition Herodotus records identifies this as a divine deliverance through mice — a rationalized account of a sudden military withdrawal. The bubonic plague theory (mice as plague carriers; rapid mass death overnight) is the most common naturalistic explanation offered. Whatever the mechanism, the dual attestation — Israelite tradition (YHWH’s angel), Egyptian/Greek tradition (mice) — corroborates the historical reality of a massive sudden Assyrian setback at Jerusalem in 701 BCE. The Sennacherib Prism’s silence on taking Jerusalem is the most significant external corroboration: the greatest military annalist of the ancient Near East (who boasted extensively of his victories) does not claim to have taken the city that his own account confirms he besieged.
1:
v1 — Hezekiah tears his clothes and goes to the temple
Hezekiah's response to the crisis is immediate and characteristic: he tears his clothes (va-yiqra et begadav), puts on sackcloth (va-yitkas ba-saq), and goes to the house of the LORD. The sequence of mourning-gestures (torn garments, sackcloth, temple) is the classic OT response to national emergency — the same pattern as Josiah's response to the law-book discovery (2 Kings 22:11-20). The temple as the first recourse in crisis reflects the centralization of worship Hezekiah himself accomplished; he goes to the place he has designated as the sole legitimate worship site.
2:
v2 — The delegation to Isaiah: senior priests in sackcloth
Hezekiah sends Eliakim, Shebna, and the senior priests (ha-kohanim ha-zeqenim) — all wrapped in sackcloth — to Isaiah son of Amoz. The prophetic consultation in national emergency was a standard institution in ancient Israel (cf. 1 Kings 22:5-28; 2 Chronicles 18:4). Isaiah's position in Jerusalem gave him privileged access to the royal court; Isaiah 1-39 describes his extended ministry during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah. The parallel account in Isaiah 36-39 is almost verbatim to 2 Kings 18-20, with the significant addition of Hezekiah's thanksgiving psalm (Isaiah 38:9-20).
3:
v3 — A day of distress, rebuke, and disgrace; children at the birth-point
Hezekiah's message to Isaiah describes the crisis using the metaphor of a woman in labor who lacks strength to deliver (u-koach ein le-leidah). The metaphor appears in other crisis contexts (Isaiah 37:3; cf. Isaiah 13:8; 2 Kings 26:17-18; Micah 4:9-10) and captures the moment when the crisis is most acute but resolution has not come — the pain is at maximum but the outcome uncertain. The three words — tzarah (distress), tokhechah (rebuke/chastisement), and ne'atzah (contempt/disgrace) — describe the multi-layered crisis: external military threat, possible divine rebuke, and national humiliation.
4:
v4 — 'Perhaps the LORD your God will rebuke the living God's mocker'
Hezekiah's petition to Isaiah includes the phrase Elohei chay (the living God) — a title that directly addresses the Rabshakeh's challenge. The contrast is explicit: the gods of Hamath and Arpad are not living gods (v18: they are wood and stone); YHWH is Elohim chay — alive, capable of hearing and responding. The careful phrase 'ulay yishma YHWH Elohekha' — 'perhaps the LORD your God will hear' — is a petition hedged with appropriate humility, not presumption, which is theologically significant: Hezekiah does not assume he deserves divine intervention.
5:
v5 — The servants come to Isaiah
The brief transitional verse confirms the delegation has arrived. The simplicity of va-yavo'u avdei ha-melekh Chizkiyahu el Yeshayahu — 'the servants of King Hezekiah came to Isaiah' — marks the movement from palace to prophet. In the narrative structure, this creates a pivot: the human political response (Hezekiah's submission and distress) now meets the prophetic word. Isaiah's response will reframe the entire crisis from a military problem to a theological one: not Judah versus Assyria but the living God versus the boastful human power.
6:
v6 — Isaiah's oracle: 'Do not be afraid of the servant-boys' words'
Isaiah's response is direct: al-tira (do not be afraid). The oracle diminishes the Rabshakeh's status — the word na'ar (young men/servant-boys) rather than officials or generals — expressing prophetic contempt for the Rabshakeh's speech. The phrase “the words of the servant-boys of the king of Assyria who blasphemed me” — nidafu (blasphemed/mocked) — frames the speech as blasphemy against YHWH, not merely political challenge to Judah. The classification of the Rabshakeh's speech as divine blasphemy establishes the terms for the divine response: it is not a political problem but a theological one that God will address directly.
7:
v7 — 'I will put a spirit in him and he will return and fall by the sword'
The divine prediction: God will put a ruach (spirit/wind/impulse) in Sennacherib causing him to hear a rumor (shemu'ah) and return to his land, where he will fall by the sword. This prediction is fulfilled in vv36-37: Sennacherib does return (triggered by v9's rumor about Tirhakah), and is killed by his sons in his homeland. The ruach is not a spirit of prophecy but of distraction — an inner impulse that redirects the king's attention. The word shemu'ah (report/rumor) is the same word used in v9 for the intelligence about Tirhakah. The divine mechanism is the providential use of political events.
8:
v8 — Rabshakeh returns to find Sennacherib at Libnah
The Rabshakeh returns and finds Sennacherib has moved from Lachish to Libnah — a city in the Shephelah (Joshua 10:29-30; 2 Kings 15:42), probably modern Tell Burna or Tell es-Safi (Gath). The movement from Lachish to Libnah suggests Sennacherib was continuing military operations in the lowlands while the Jerusalem delegation was underway. The siege of Libnah was concurrent with the Rabshakeh's diplomatic mission, giving context to the complexity of Sennacherib's 701 campaign: multiple cities being engaged simultaneously.
9:
v9 — Tirhakah king of Ethiopia: the chronological problem
When Sennacherib hears that Tirhakah king of Ethiopia (Tirhaqah melekh Kush) has come out to fight him, he sends a second letter to Hezekiah. Tirhakah (Taharqa in Egyptian) was the third king of Egypt's 25th (Kushite) dynasty, reigning 690–664 BCE. The chronological problem: Sennacherib's 701 BCE campaign predates Tirhakah's reign by 11 years. Scholars propose: (1) he commanded forces before becoming king (he would have been c.19 at the time — an early command role is plausible); (2) his title reflects his later royal status; (3) there was a second Sennacherib campaign. The issue remains debated.
10:
v10 — The second letter: 'Don't let your God deceive you'
Sennacherib's second communication shifts from the public oratory of the Rabshakeh to a written letter (sefarim). The letter claims Hezekiah's God is deceiving him — yashia'kha Elohekha (your God is misleading you). The letter reuses the Rabshakeh's language of deception/misleading. The shift from oral proclamation to written document is significant in the history of political communication — the letter allows the message to be read privately and repeatedly, creating different psychological pressure than the public shout. Hezekiah will take this letter to the temple and spread it before the LORD (v14) — treating a political document as a theological challenge.
11:
v11 — 'You have heard what Assyrian kings did — utterly destroying all nations'
The historical claim: the kings of Assyria have hachatem (devoted to destruction/utterly destroyed) all the nations. The word hacharam is the same root as the Israelite concept of cherem (ban/devotion to destruction) — the Rabshakeh's letter appropriates the covenantal destruction-language to describe Assyrian conquest. Whether this reflects deliberate irony (using Israel's holy war vocabulary) or simply the fact that destruction is destruction regardless of who performs it, the effect is to cast Assyrian conquest in the same totalizing terms as the Canaanite conquest.
12:
v12 — Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, Eden in Telassar — no god delivered them
The catalog of conquered regions: Gozan (Tell Halaf, Khabur River area — confirmed as Assyrian territory by cuneiform records), Haran (the famous city on the Balikh River, significant for Abraham's departure — Genesis 11:31-12:1), Rezeph (Rasappa in Assyrian sources, modern Resafa in Syria), and the people of Eden in Telassar (possibly the Bit-Adini, an Aramaean tribal state on the middle Euphrates, conquered by Shalmaneser III). Each name in the list is identifiable from Assyrian cuneiform records, confirming the historical accuracy of the catalog.
13:
v13 — Kings of Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim — where are they?
The rhetorical question echoes the Rabshakeh's earlier list (2 Kings 18:34). Hamath had an anti-Assyrian king Yau-bi'di who was defeated by Sargon II (720 BCE); Arpad was conquered by Tiglath-Pileser III (740 BCE). The question “where are the kings?” implies they no longer exist as independent rulers — they have been removed, executed, or absorbed into the Assyrian provincial system. The implied answer: they are gone. The implicit challenge: Hezekiah will be next unless he submits. The list of fallen kings is designed to project historical inevitability onto Judah's situation.
14:
v14 — Hezekiah spreads the letter before the LORD in the temple
Va-yifros Chizkiyahu et ha-sefarim lifnei YHWH — 'Hezekiah spread out the letters before the LORD.' This act — taking a political document and laying it open before God — is one of the most vivid acts of prayer in the OT. It treats YHWH as a present king who can read the letter, see the threat, and respond to it. The act presupposes that the political crisis is fundamentally a theological confrontation: Sennacherib’s letter is not primarily addressed to Hezekiah but to YHWH. The temple becomes the throne room of the true King who will judge the matter.
15:
v15 — Hezekiah's prayer: 'You alone are God over all kingdoms'
Hezekiah's prayer begins with the divine address: YHWH Elohei Yisrael yoshev ha-keruvim — 'LORD God of Israel, enthroned on the cherubim.' The cherub-throne is the ark of the covenant's imagery (1 Samuel 4:4; 2 Samuel 6:2; Psalms 80:1; 2 Kings 99:1) — the place where YHWH is conceived as enthroned in the heavenly throne room, represented on earth by the ark. Then the crucial monotheistic claim: attah-hu ha-Elohim levadekha le-khol mamlakhot ha-aretz — 'you alone are God over all the kingdoms of the earth.' This directly answers the Rabshakeh's implicit comparative theology: YHWH is not one god among others but the sole God of all nations.
16:
v16 — 'Incline your ear and hear; open your eyes and see'
The petition uses the classical language of divine attention: hatteh YHWH oznekha u-shma (incline your ear and hear) and pekach YHWH einekha ur'eh (open your eyes and see). The anthropomorphic language is common in petitionary prayer (Psalms 17:6; 2 Kings 31:2; 2 Kings 71:2; 2 Kings 86:1) — it presupposes a God who can choose to attend or not, and who must be petitioned to direct his attention to the current crisis. The specific objects of divine attention: Sennacherib's words (kol divrei Sennacherib) and his mockery of the living God (asher shalach le-charef Elohim chay).
17:
v17 — 'It is true, LORD, that the Assyrian kings have destroyed the nations'
Hezekiah's prayer includes a remarkable concession: achen YHWH — 'it is true, LORD.' He does not dispute the historical facts the Rabshakeh cited. Yes, the Assyrian kings destroyed nation after nation. The prayer is not based on a denial of Assyria's military record but on a theological distinction: what made those conquests possible is different from what Judah is relying on. The concession of historical fact before making the theological distinction is rhetorically honest — Hezekiah is not appealing to divine help on the grounds that Assyria is militarily weak, but on the grounds that YHWH is categorically different.
18:
v18 — 'Their gods were wood and stone — no gods at all'
The explanation: the Assyrian kings defeated those nations and cast their gods into the fire — ki lo Elohim hemah ki im ma'aseh yedei adam etz va-aven — 'because they were not gods at all, merely the work of human hands, wood and stone.' This is the classic OT polemic against idolatry (cf. Isaiah 44:9-20; Psalms 115:4-8; Deuteronomy 4:28). The argument is logical: Assyria defeated those nations because their gods were no gods. But YHWH is not the work of human hands. The defeat of the idol-worshiping nations cannot be extrapolated to the defeat of the living God's people — the comparison breaks down at a categorical level.
19:
v19 — 'Deliver us so all the kingdoms may know you alone are God'
The climax of Hezekiah's petition: hoshine'u mi-yado (deliver us from his hand) — not for Judah's sake primarily, but veyede'u kol-mamlakhot ha-aretz ki attah YHWH Elohim levadekha — 'so that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you, LORD, are God alone.' The universal scope of the requested deliverance is striking: Hezekiah prays for a miracle whose purpose is global theological testimony. This transforms the siege of Jerusalem from a local military crisis into a cosmic theological event — the vindication of monotheism before the watching nations.
20:
v20 — Isaiah's oracle response: 'I have heard your prayer'
Isaiah sends word to Hezekiah: shama'ti — 'I have heard.' The divine response to Hezekiah's temple prayer is immediate — the prayer is heard (the very thing the temple dedication in 1 Kings 8 repeatedly prayed for: 'hear in heaven and forgive'). The response comes through the prophet Isaiah, whose oracle (vv21-34) is one of the most sustained prophetic poems in the OT, combining taunting address to Sennacherib and promise of deliverance to Judah. The parallel in Isaiah 37 is nearly identical.
21:
v21 — The virgin daughter of Zion despises and mocks Sennacherib
The oracle opens with a personified Zion (betulat bat-Tzion — virgin daughter of Zion) despising and mocking Sennacherib, shaking her head behind him. The image of a city as a daughter/young woman is common in prophetic literature (Isaiah 1:8; 2 Kings 16:1; 2 Kings 23:12; Jeremiah 46:11; Lamentations 1:15). Here Zion is not prostrate before the conqueror but contemptuous — a reversal of the expected posture of a besieged city. The poetry begins with the defeated image of Sennacherib’s expectations and immediately inverts it: Jerusalem has not bowed, and in the prophetic vision it is Sennacherib who is reduced to an object of contempt.
22:
v22 — 'You have mocked the Holy One of Israel'
The oracle identifies the true target of Sennacherib's campaign: et mi cheraftah ve-gidafta... et Qedosh Yisrael — 'whom have you mocked and blasphemed? The Holy One of Israel.' Qedosh Yisrael (the Holy One of Israel) is a characteristically Isaianic divine title — it appears approximately 25 times in Isaiah (both 1-39 and 40-66) and rarely elsewhere. The title emphasizes divine transcendence and separateness from all that is merely human and finite. Sennacherib's arrogance has not merely challenged Judah — he has blasphemed the categorically different God of all creation.
23:
v23 — Sennacherib's boast: I climbed to Lebanon's heights by my messengers
The oracle voices Sennacherib's boasting in first person: through messengers he claims to have climbed the highest mountains, felled Lebanon’s cedars and cypresses, and reached Carmel's heights and the waters of the rivers. The boasting reflects actual Assyrian royal ideology: Assyrian annals are full of first-person royal boasting (ana rabuti biti Ashur — “at the great command of Assur”) claiming divine sanction for military achievements. The imagery of cutting down the tallest trees (cedar of Lebanon) is also used metaphorically in Assyrian texts for defeating the greatest kings.
24:
v24 — 'I dug wells and drank foreign water; I dried up Egypt's streams'
The boast continues: crossing deserts by digging cisterns, drying up Egypt's Nile channels with the soles of his feet. These are hyperbolic claims of world-dominating power — the ability to traverse the desert (a classic Assyrian military challenge) and drain Egypt's agricultural lifeline. The specific mention of Egypt is significant: Sennacherib never actually conquered Egypt (that was Esarhaddon in 671 BCE and Ashurbanipal), but the oracle voices the claim as it would be made by an Assyrian great king with maximum ambition.
25:
v25 — 'Have you not heard? I ordained long ago what I would do'
God's response to Sennacherib's boasting: ha-lo shama'ta — 'have you not heard?' The question implies Sennacherib should have known but did not. Then the theological reframe: God determined long ago (me-yamim qedem) what Sennacherib would do; from ancient times (mi-qedem) it was planned. Sennacherib’s conquests are not his achievement but God’s purpose working through him — an Isaianic theme (cf. Isaiah 10:5-19: Assyria as the rod of God’s anger). The Assyrian empire was not the autonomous achievement of Assyrian military power but the instrument of divine historical purpose.
26:
v26 — The conquered peoples were powerless like grass on rooftops
The conquered nations had “little power” (qetzrei yad — short of hand/powerless) because God had already determined they would be defeated. They are compared to esev sadeh (grass of the field) and yereq deshe (green shoots) — specifically like grass on rooftops (deshot geggot), which sprouts quickly in morning dew but is scorched before it matures. The rooftop-grass image is used in Psalms 129:6 for enemies that will be withered. The nations fell not because Assyria was strong but because God had decreed their overthrow.
27:
v27 — 'I know your dwelling, your going out and coming in, and your rage'
YHWH’s omniscience is asserted: vedam I know your dwelling (shivtekha) and your going out (tzet'kha) and your coming in (bo'ekha). God’s knowledge of Sennacherib’s movements and inner life (the rigzekha elai — your rage against me) positions the divine as the all-seeing sovereign over the Assyrian king’s entire existence. The contrast with Sennacherib’s boasting (I climbed the mountains; I dried up the rivers) is sharp: what Sennacherib sees as his own autonomous achievement, God sees as fully known and supervised.
28:
v28 — The hook in the nose: Sennacherib will be sent back
Ve-samtiy chachiy be-appekha u-metsiltiy be-sefatekha — 'I will put my hook in your nose and my bridle in your mouth.' The image of hook-and-bridle control is drawn from Assyrian military iconography: Assyrian reliefs show conquered kings led by hooks through their noses or lips. God appropriates this Assyrian image of conquest and applies it to Sennacherib himself — the conqueror will be controlled and redirected like a captured animal. Va-hashivotikha ba-derekh asher bata bah — 'I will send you back the way you came' — the oracle promises a reversal without a battle.
29:
v29 — The agricultural sign: three-year recovery sequence
A sign (ot) for Hezekiah: this year eat what grows on its own (saphiach — volunteer growth from unharvested grain), next year what springs from that (sachiach — growth from the saphiach), and in the third year sow, reap, plant vineyards, and eat their fruit. The three-year sign implies that agricultural disruption from the siege will take two years to overcome before normal cultivation resumes. The gradual restoration — volunteer growth, then secondary volunteer, then deliberate planting — reflects the actual agricultural recovery timeline after military disruption. Normal farming requires safety and stability; the sign promises enough time will pass for recovery.
30:
v30 — The surviving remnant will take root downward and bear fruit upward
Ve-yosefah peletah beit Yehudah ha-nishe'arah shoresh lemattah ve-asah feri lema'lah — 'The surviving remnant of the house of Judah will again take root downward and bear fruit upward.' The remnant (she'erit) theology is central to Isaiah — a faithful remnant will survive judgment and become the seedbed of restoration (cf. Isaiah 10:20-22; 2 Kings 11:11-16; 2 Kings 37:31-32). The root/fruit imagery speaks of organic growth from small beginnings: what looked like a withered stump will send down deep roots and bear fruit. This is the same imagery Isaiah uses for the shoot from the stump of Jesse (Isaiah 11:1).
31:
v31 — 'The zeal of the LORD of hosts will accomplish this'
Ki min-Yerushalayim tetzei she'erit u-mi-har-Tzion peletah qin'at YHWH tzeva'ot ta'aseh zot — 'For a remnant will come out from Jerusalem and survivors from Mount Zion. The zeal of the LORD of hosts will accomplish this.' The identical phrase qin'at YHWH tzeva'ot (the zeal of the LORD of hosts) appears in Isaiah 9:7 — the oracle about the prince of peace whose kingdom grows forever. The zeal that establishes the eternal Davidic kingdom is the same divine energy that drives the current deliverance. The phrase connects the immediate historical promise to the larger eschatological horizon.
32:
v32 — Sennacherib will not enter the city or shoot an arrow here
The oracle specifies Sennacherib's defeat in technical military terms: he will not enter the city, shoot an arrow here, come before it with a shield, or cast up a siege ramp (loh-yishpokh aleha solelah). The siege ramp (solelah) was the standard Assyrian siege technique — a massive earthen ramp built up against city walls to allow assault troops to breach the fortifications. The Lachish relief shows exactly this technique being used at Lachish. Jerusalem will be spared this very thing — no arrow, no shield, no ramp, no entry. The promise is absolute and specific.
33:
v33 — 'He will go back the same way he came and not enter this city'
The symmetry of departure and arrival: ba-derekh asher-ba bah yashuv — 'by the way he came he will return.' No siege, no assault, no conquest — Sennacherib will simply reverse course. The fulfillment is recorded in v36: va-yetze va-yelekh va-yashuv Sennacherib — 'Sennacherib broke camp, went away, and returned.' The Sennacherib Annals do not describe a defeat or retreat — they claim a successful siege and tribute payment, but conspicuously stop short of claiming Jerusalem's conquest (unlike all the other cities in the campaign). The Assyrian record’s silence about Jerusalem’s fall is itself evidence of something unexpected occurring.
34:
v34 — 'I will defend this city for my own sake and for David's sake'
Ve-ganoti al ha-ir ha-zot lehoshi'ah le-ma'ani u-le-ma'an David avdi — 'I will defend this city to save it, for my own sake and for the sake of my servant David.' Two reasons: divine honor (le-ma'ani — for my own sake, my name, my reputation) and Davidic covenant (le-ma'an David avdi). The divine honor motive reflects the Isaianic concern for YHWH’s name among the nations — the question raised by the Rabshakeh (which of the gods rescued his land?) will be answered by YHWH’s defense of Jerusalem. The Davidic covenant motive reflects the Deuteronomistic faith that God’s commitment to David’s dynasty transcends the failures of individual kings.
36:
v36 — Sennacherib breaks camp and returns to Nineveh
Va-yisa va-yelekh va-yashuv Sennacherib — 'Sennacherib broke camp and went and returned to Nineveh.' The three verbs are terse and definitive: he lifted camp, went, returned. No conquest of Jerusalem, no triumph. Sennacherib’s own Annals describe the campaign’s Judean portion as successful in terms of tribute and captured cities but make no claim to taking Jerusalem itself — they say he “shut up Hezekiah in Jerusalem like a bird in a cage.” The cage metaphor implies encirclement, not conquest. The biblical account and the Assyrian Annals agree on the basic fact: Jerusalem was not taken.
37:
v37 — Sennacherib killed by his sons in Nineveh; Esarhaddon succeeds
While worshiping in the temple of Nisroch (Nisrokh) his god, Sennacherib is killed by his sons Adrammelech (Adrammelekh) and Sharezer (Shar'etzer), who flee to Ararat (Urartu). Esarhaddon (Esar-Chaddon) his son then becomes king. This is confirmed by the Babylonian Chronicle: Sennacherib was indeed murdered by one of his sons in 681 BCE, approximately 20 years after the 701 campaign. The Assyrian sources describe a succession crisis; the biblical account attributes the act to two specific sons. Esarhaddon (681–669 BCE) then reunified the empire and later conquered Egypt in 671 BCE. The god Nisroch is uncertain — possibly Nusku, the fire god, or a variant of Marduk or Ninurta.
